IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important and practical problem—capturing discourse-level diversity in neural dialog—which is a recognized limitation of standard encoder–decoder models. Introducing a CVAE framework with a knowledge-guided conditioning (kgCVAE) and pragmatic training fixes (BOW loss and KL annealing) provides a concrete, reproducible path to produce diverse, interpretable responses and appears validated on a realistic dataset (Switchboard). While this builds on existing VAE ideas rather than overturning them, the combination of explicit discourse conditioning and training strategy is likely to change how researchers approach diversity in dialog systems and to be adopted or extended in follow-up work. | B(4): This paper tackles the important problem of capturing discourse-level diversity in neural dialog, proposing a CVAE framework (and a knowledge-guided kgCVAE) that explicitly links latent variables to interpretable linguistic labels and demonstrates improved diversity on Switchboard. The combination of predictive y supervision and a BOW loss/KL-annealing recipe addresses a well-known optimization bottleneck (vanishing latent variable) in a way that is likely to influence subsequent work on controllable and interpretable latent dialog models. While building on existing CVAE approaches, the focus on discourse-level decision-making and label interpretability meaningfully advances practical dialog modeling and opens clear directions for follow-up research and application.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important and practical problem—capturing discourse-level diversity in neural dialog—which is a recognized limitation of standard encoder–decoder models. While this builds on existing VAE ideas rather than overturning them, the combination of explicit discourse conditioning and training strategy is likely to change how researchers approach diversity in dialog systems and to be adopted or extended in follow-up work.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—neural dialog generation—with a methodologically relevant contribution (CVAE + kgCVAE) and concrete training improvements (KL annealing, BOW loss) evaluated on a standard corpus (Switchboard). The experimental setup and metrics (BLEU, embedding-based diversity) and focus on discourse-level diversity target the right community and expectations for a top-tier venue. While I would later require thorough baselines and reproducibility details to judge scientific quality, based on the provided summary the work is well positioned and appropriate for the conference audience. | B(5): This paper squarely addresses core NLP/ML conference interests: neural dialog modeling, latent-variable methods (CVAE/kgCVAE), and discourse-level interpretability, and it evaluates on a standard conversational corpus (Switchboard). The methodological contributions (conditioning z on explicit discourse features y, BOW loss and KL annealing to mitigate posterior collapse) and comparison to an encoder–decoder baseline align with community expectations, and the discussion of limitations and discourse interpretability makes it well targeted to the venue’s audience. While evaluation relies on BLEU and embedding-based diversity metrics (which have known limits), the work’s focus on discourse-level decision-making and model interpretability is highly relevant for top-tier review.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—neural dialog generation—with a methodologically relevant contribution (CVAE + kgCVAE) and concrete training improvements (KL annealing, BOW loss) evaluated on a standard corpus (Switchboard). The experimental setup and metrics (BLEU, embedding-based diversity) and focus on discourse-level diversity target the right community and expectations for a top-tier venue. While I would later require thorough baselines and reproducibility details to judge scientific quality, based on the provided summary the work is well positioned and appropriate for the conference audience.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper squarely addresses core NLP/ML conference interests: neural dialog modeling, latent-variable methods (CVAE/kgCVAE), and discourse-level interpretability, and it evaluates on a standard conversational corpus (Switchboard). The methodological contributions (conditioning z on explicit discourse features y, BOW loss and KL annealing to mitigate posterior collapse) and comparison to an encoder–decoder baseline align with community expectations, and the discussion of limitations and discourse interpretability makes it well targeted to the venue’s audience. While evaluation relies on BLEU and embedding-based diversity metrics (which have known limits), the work’s focus on discourse-level decision-making and model interpretability is highly relevant for top-tier review.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper applies conditional VAEs to model discourse-level diversity in dialog and introduces a knowledge-guided variant (kgCVAE) that conditions the latent space on explicit linguistic labels and reconstructs them — a clear, non-trivial extension beyond standard encoder–decoder and diversity-promoting objectives (e.g., Li et al.). The training fixes (BOW loss + KL annealing) are sensible combinations of known techniques but their targeted use for preventing posterior collapse in dialog CVAEs and for making z carry discourse-level information is a useful engineering and methodological contribution. Overall this is a notable, well-differentiated advance rather than a wholly new paradigm, so it merits a high-but-not-maximal originality score. | B(4): The paper offers a notable and distinguishable extension of CVAE-based dialog modeling by introducing a knowledge-guided variant (kgCVAE) that conditions on and reconstructs explicit discourse/linguistic labels (y), yielding interpretable high-level outputs — a clear novelty over prior encoder-decoder and diversity-promoting objectives (e.g., Li et al. 2016) which do not integrate explicit discourse signals into the latent variable. The training innovations (bag-of-words loss plus KL annealing) address known VAE optimization pathologies in NLG and meaningfully improve latent encoding of discourse-level phenomena. While the core use of latent variables for diversity is building on existing VAE/CVAE work, the combination of discourse supervision, interpretability, and training tricks constitutes a non-trivial and informative advance that would interest experts in dialogue representation and controllable generation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies conditional VAEs to model discourse-level diversity in dialog and introduces a knowledge-guided variant (kgCVAE) that conditions the latent space on explicit linguistic labels and reconstructs them — a clear, non-trivial extension beyond standard encoder–decoder and diversity-promoting objectives (e.g., Li et al.). The training fixes (BOW loss + KL annealing) are sensible combinations of known techniques but their targeted use for preventing posterior collapse in dialog CVAEs and for making z carry discourse-level information is a useful engineering and methodological contribution. Overall this is a notable, well-differentiated advance rather than a wholly new paradigm, so it merits a high-but-not-maximal originality score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable and distinguishable extension of CVAE-based dialog modeling by introducing a knowledge-guided variant (kgCVAE) that conditions on and reconstructs explicit discourse/linguistic labels (y), yielding interpretable high-level outputs — a clear novelty over prior encoder-decoder and diversity-promoting objectives (e.g., Li et al. 2016) which do not integrate explicit discourse signals into the latent variable. The training innovations (bag-of-words loss plus KL annealing) address known VAE optimization pathologies in NLG and meaningfully improve latent encoding of discourse-level phenomena. While the core use of latent variables for diversity is building on existing VAE/CVAE work, the combination of discourse supervision, interpretability, and training tricks constitutes a non-trivial and informative advance that would interest experts in dialogue representation and controllable generation.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear technical claims and provides experiments on Switchboard showing increased response diversity and a kgCVAE variant with interpretable labels; the use of BOW loss and KL annealing to mitigate the vanishing latent problem is plausible and empirically validated. However, from the summary I see gaps in experimental rigor: only one baseline (standard encoder–decoder) is compared, statistical significance/error bars are not mentioned, and reproducibility details (code, seeds, detailed ablations isolating each component and comparable hyperparameter/decoding budgets) appear missing. The mathematical framing of the CVAE is standard and seems correct, but stronger controls and diagnostics (more baselines, ablations, and statistical tests) are needed to fully justify all conclusions. Overall the work is technically solid with minor methodological limitations that do not overturn the main claims. | B(4): The paper's main claims (CVAE and kgCVAE architectures, use of BOW loss and KL annealing to mitigate latent-variable collapse, and improved diversity on Switchboard versus an encoder–decoder baseline) are supported by experiments as summarized. The technical approach builds on standard CVAE machinery and no incorrect mathematical derivations are reported, but the summary contains no detailed proofs to check. Experimental methodology is broadly sound (realistic dataset, sensible baseline), but evaluation relies on surface metrics (BLEU, bag-of-words cosine) with no mention of statistical testing or stronger human/pragmatic evaluations to substantiate claims about discourse-level decision-making and interpretability of y; this is an unsupported leap. Overall the results justify the core claim that latent-variable models increase output diversity, but stronger evaluation would be required to fully validate the discourse-level and interpretability assertions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear technical claims and provides experiments on Switchboard showing increased response diversity and a kgCVAE variant with interpretable labels; the use of BOW loss and KL annealing to mitigate the vanishing latent problem is plausible and empirically validated. However, from the summary I see gaps in experimental rigor: only one baseline (standard encoder–decoder) is compared, statistical significance/error bars are not mentioned, and reproducibility details (code, seeds, detailed ablations isolating each component and comparable hyperparameter/decoding budgets) appear missing. The mathematical framing of the CVAE is standard and seems correct, but stronger controls and diagnostics (more baselines, ablations, and statistical tests) are needed to fully justify all conclusions. Overall the work is technically solid with minor methodological limitations that do not overturn the main claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims (CVAE and kgCVAE architectures, use of BOW loss and KL annealing to mitigate latent-variable collapse, and improved diversity on Switchboard versus an encoder–decoder baseline) are supported by experiments as summarized. The technical approach builds on standard CVAE machinery and no incorrect mathematical derivations are reported, but the summary contains no detailed proofs to check. Experimental methodology is broadly sound (realistic dataset, sensible baseline), but evaluation relies on surface metrics (BLEU, bag-of-words cosine) with no mention of statistical testing or stronger human/pragmatic evaluations to substantiate claims about discourse-level decision-making and interpretability of y; this is an unsupported leap. Overall the results justify the core claim that latent-variable models increase output diversity, but stronger evaluation would be required to fully validate the discourse-level and interpretability assertions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized with a clear intro→methods→experiments→conclusion flow and explicitly states its main contributions early (CVAE, kgCVAE, and training fixes). Notation appears consistent and the writing quality is described as good; related work coverage and hyperparameter reporting support reproducibility. Figures, tables and examples are reported as effective, though from a reproduction-focused perspective I note minor presentation gaps (e.g., no mention of released code or detailed pseudo-code for training schedules) that prevent it being exemplary. Given these small shortcomings, the presentation quality is very good but not flawless. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from problem motivation to method (CVAE and kgCVAE), experiments on Switchboard, and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and reinforced in the methodology summary. Notation and methodological description seem consistent and well-defined (including details like KL annealing and BOW loss), and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective and informative. Writing is generally clear and grammatically sound, though describing VAE optimization challenges suggests some sections require careful reading for full comprehension, so presentation is strong but with minor clarity hotspots.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized with a clear intro→methods→experiments→conclusion flow and explicitly states its main contributions early (CVAE, kgCVAE, and training fixes). MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against a standard encoder–decoder baseline (akin to Serban et al., 2016) on the same Switchboard data and using comparable automatic metrics, and it includes useful ablations (BOW loss, KL annealing, kgCVAE) that help isolate contributions. However, it omits empirical comparisons to other explicit diversity-promoting approaches (e.g., Li et al., 2016’s diversity objective) even though those are highly relevant to the same problem. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly and acknowledge optimization challenges, but the experimental scope could be strengthened by adding those competing baselines to ensure a fully comprehensive comparison. | B(3): The paper compares its CVAE and kgCVAE models against an encoder–decoder baseline (similar to Serban et al., 2016a) on the same Switchboard data and evaluation metrics, so comparisons are fair in setup. However, it omits empirical comparisons to other strong, directly relevant diversity-focused baselines (e.g., diversity-promoting objective methods such as Li et al., 2016) despite discussing related work, which weakens claims of superiority. The paper analyzes differences in model design and training (BOW loss, KL annealing) and discusses optimization limitations, but it does not clearly acknowledge or empirically test whether those alternative approaches perform comparably. Overall the comparisons are reasonable but miss important competing baselines.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against a standard encoder–decoder baseline (akin to Serban et al., 2016) on the same Switchboard data and using comparable automatic metrics, and it includes useful ablations (BOW loss, KL annealing, kgCVAE) that help isolate contributions. However, it omits empirical comparisons to other explicit diversity-promoting approaches (e.g., Li et al., 2016’s diversity objective) even though those are highly relevant to the same problem. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly and acknowledge optimization challenges, but the experimental scope could be strengthened by adding those competing baselines to ensure a fully comprehensive comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its CVAE and kgCVAE models against an encoder–decoder baseline (similar to Serban et al., 2016a) on the same Switchboard data and evaluation metrics, so comparisons are fair in setup. However, it omits empirical comparisons to other strong, directly relevant diversity-focused baselines (e.g., diversity-promoting objective methods such as Li et al., 2016) despite discussing related work, which weakens claims of superiority. The paper analyzes differences in model design and training (BOW loss, KL annealing) and discusses optimization limitations, but it does not clearly acknowledge or empirically test whether those alternative approaches perform comparably. Overall the comparisons are reasonable but miss important competing baselines.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents plausible methodology (CVAE and kgCVAE, BOW loss + KL annealing) and evaluates on Switchboard against a reasonable encoder–decoder baseline with diversity metrics, so the core claims are supported to an extent. However, from the summary I see no explicit ablation studies isolating the contributions of BOW loss, KL annealing, and kgCVAE conditioning, nor evidence of statistical significance or robustness (seeds, splits, or released code) which weakens reproducibility. Related work appears thoroughly covered and the authors do acknowledge training difficulties and limitations, but the experimental analysis is missing key sensitivity/diagnostic probes I would expect to fully validate the claims. | B(4): The paper presents a coherent CVAE and kgCVAE approach with training fixes (BOW loss, KL annealing) and evaluates on Switchboard showing improved response diversity versus an encoder–decoder baseline; hyperparameters are reported and discourse-oriented labels y are explicitly modeled for interpretability. However, the experiments appear to lack key ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of BOW loss vs KL annealing vs latent size, and ablation of the kgCVAE’s y-conditioning) and do not report human judgments of discourse appropriateness or comparisons to other diversity-promoting objectives (e.g., Li et al., 2016). Related work coverage is noted as thorough and the paper explicitly discusses optimization limitations (vanishing latent variable, data requirements), but reproducibility would be strengthened by released code and more extensive evaluation of discourse-level correctness beyond BLEU/BoW metrics. Overall the empirical evidence is solid but missing some analyses crucial for claims about discourse-level decision-making and interpretability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents plausible methodology (CVAE and kgCVAE, BOW loss + KL annealing) and evaluates on Switchboard against a reasonable encoder–decoder baseline with diversity metrics, so the core claims are supported to an extent. However, from the summary I see no explicit ablation studies isolating the contributions of BOW loss, KL annealing, and kgCVAE conditioning, nor evidence of statistical significance or robustness (seeds, splits, or released code) which weakens reproducibility. Related work appears thoroughly covered and the authors do acknowledge training difficulties and limitations, but the experimental analysis is missing key sensitivity/diagnostic probes I would expect to fully validate the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a coherent CVAE and kgCVAE approach with training fixes (BOW loss, KL annealing) and evaluates on Switchboard showing improved response diversity versus an encoder–decoder baseline; hyperparameters are reported and discourse-oriented labels y are explicitly modeled for interpretability. However, the experiments appear to lack key ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of BOW loss vs KL annealing vs latent size, and ablation of the kgCVAE’s y-conditioning) and do not report human judgments of discourse appropriateness or comparisons to other diversity-promoting objectives (e.g., Li et al., 2016). Related work coverage is noted as thorough and the paper explicitly discusses optimization limitations (vanishing latent variable, data requirements), but reproducibility would be strengthened by released code and more extensive evaluation of discourse-level correctness beyond BLEU/BoW metrics. Overall the empirical evidence is solid but missing some analyses crucial for claims about discourse-level decision-making and interpretability.

main_claims: Introduce a CVAE-based neural dialog model that captures discourse-level diversity via latent variables, enabling generation of diverse responses with greedy decoding.

Propose a knowledge-guided CVAE (kgCVAE) variant that conditions the latent variable on explicitly extracted linguistic/discourse features (y) to better encode discourse-level information and output interpretable high-level labels (e.g., dialog acts).

Improve CVAE training for natural language generation by introducing a bag-of-word (BOW) loss and using KL annealing, addressing optimization difficulties (vanishing latent variable) and producing better latent representations.
Demonstrate on Switchboard that the proposed models generate significantly more diverse responses than a baseline encoder-decoder and exhibit competence in discourse-level decision-making.
_summary.methodology: Use a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) with a latent variable z conditioned on dialog context c (and optionally explicit linguistic features y in kgCVAE) to model a distribution over conversational intents; train end-to-end with KL annealing and an additional bag-of-word loss, and in kgCVAE predict y from z and c to guide decoding.
baselines: Encoder-decoder neural dialog model without latent variables (similar to Serban et al., 2016a)
limitations: Training CVAE is a challenging optimization problem and often requires large amounts of data (stated explicitly).
The vanishing latent variable problem: straightforward VAE with RNN decoder tends to ignore the latent variable (stated explicitly).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - neural dialog / conversational response generation

reviewer_a.name: Methodical Empiricist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating neural NLP systems in industry and academia; experienced reproducing research and deploying conversation models.
traits: insists on strong, well-tuned baselines and comparable experimental conditions (hyperparameters, search budgets, decoding strategies)
prioritizes ablation studies and diagnostic experiments that isolate contributions of individual components (e.g., BOW loss, KL annealing, kgCVAE conditioning)
values reproducibility: requests training details, random seeds, data splits, code or pseudo-code for model and training schedule
looks for robust quantitative evaluation including statistically-sound diversity/quality metrics and error bars, and probes for failure modes
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments convincingly support the claims and whether others can reproduce and build on the work.

reviewer_b.name: Discourse Systems Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor researching dialog systems and computational discourse with a track record of work on dialogue acts, controllable generation, and interpretability in conversational models.
traits: examines novelty relative to prior CVAE and latent-variable dialogue work and how this paper advances discourse-level modeling
values linguistic interpretability and whether latent variables or predicted labels (y) correspond to meaningful discourse phenomena (e.g., dialog acts)
assesses the practical impact on downstream dialog applications and whether evaluation reflects real-world conversational quality beyond surface diversity
notices connections to prior evaluation methodologies (human judgments, pragmatic/strategic appropriateness) and expects discussion of limitations and ethical/interactive implications
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's contribution to discourse-aware dialog modeling and the broader significance and interpretability of the proposed approach.
